rxn_class_summary: Development of a high-throughput electrochemistry platform (HTe-Chem) demonstrated on reductive ketone-olefin coupling, oxidative phenolic C-H/C-H coupling, and reductive coupling of iminium chlorides with cyanopyridines to give hindered primary amines.
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction generates ketyl radicals (from ketone 14) and α-amino radicals (from iminium chlorides 21a-d) plus cyanopyridine radical anions (22-27); in the Waldvogel oxidative C-H/C-H case, anodic ECE oxidation of phenol 17 generates a p-quinone methide that pairs with cathodically generated hexafluoroisopropoxide.

key_intermediates.name: C-centered radical after ketyl addition to allylbenzene 15
p-quinone methide electrophile from phenol 17 (ECE oxidation)
α-amino radical from cathodic reduction of iminium chloride 21
cyanopyridine radical anion (proton-coupled cathodic reduction of 22-27)

electron_flow: In the ketone-olefin coupling, the cathode reduces ketone 14 to a ketyl radical (ECEC) which adds to alkene 15; the resulting C-radical is further reduced and protonated to give 16. In the cyanopyridine coupling, the cathode independently generates an α-amino radical and a cyanopyridine radical anion that couple, then lose H+/CN- to deliver the hindered amine. In the Waldvogel paired electrolysis, the anode oxidizes phenol 17 (ECE) to a p-quinone methide while HFIP is deprotonated/reduced to hexafluoroisopropoxide; the two intermediates combine to give 20, which then undergoes TFA-promoted SN with arene 18 to give 19.
notes: Mechanisms are taken from the cited original reports (Baran/Minteer 2020 ref 49; Waldvogel 2018 ref 50; Lehnherr/Rovis 2019 ref 15) rather than newly probed here; this paper's focus is translating those reactions to the HTe-Chem high-throughput platform and screening electrode/current parameters. The benzyl hexafluoroisopropyl ether 20 is isolated as a key intermediate in the Waldvogel pathway, supporting the paired-electrolysis ECE proposal. Lower current densities (0.5 mA, 2 F) were found optimal in the amine coupling to suppress diamine side-products, consistent with the radical-radical coupling pathway.
